definitions.definition: Diagnosing failed leak check tests after A/C evacuation and recharge service. The leak check included only verifies there are no leaks at the service charge ports using a leak detecting wand or verifying dye.
definitions.details: Any additional diagnostic step beyond the service port area is not included. If the A/C system fails the leak check, troubleshooting the cause of the failure is a separate not-included operation.

definitions.definition: On vehicles that use advanced driver-assistance systems (ADAS), OEM Repair information must be consulted to ensure repairs will not negatively impact ADAS Functionality.
definitions.details: ADAS systems include forward collision warning, automatic emergency braking, lane departure warning, lane keep assist, adaptive cruise control, blind spot monitoring, parking sensors, and 360Â° camera systems. Diagnostic report documents system status before and after repairs.

definitions.definition: Following a Supplemental Restraint System (SRS) component deployment, the vehicle interior will contain a powdery residue consisting of harmless particulate by-products of the pyrotechnic charge, but may also contain traces of sodium hydroxide powder.
definitions.details: Since sodium hydroxide powder can irritate the skin, eyes, nose, or throat, wear safety glasses, rubber gloves, and long-sleeved shirt during cleanup. Process: 1) Vacuum residual powder from outside working in. 2) Vacuum heater/AC outlets while running blower on low. 3) Remove deployed SRS components. 4) Vacuum interior a second time to recover all powder.

definitions.definition: Required to maintain constant voltage during scan process. Many OEMs recommend a pre- and post-repair diagnostic scan on all vehicles involved in a collision that could reveal pre-accident or accident-related damage.
definitions.details: Please refer to OEM Position Statement and Repair Procedures for more information. Due to the variety of scan tools used, both OEM and Aftermarket, as well as the variation from vehicle to vehicle and repair to repair, MOTOR is unable to develop a time that accounts for these different scenarios. Therefore, MOTOR recommends an on-the-spot evaluation to determine an appropriate estimated time to perform diagnostic scans for each repair.

definitions.definition: Cleanup of biological hazards including vomit, body fluids, blood, animal remains, etc.
definitions.details: Bio hazard cleanup requires special cleaning/disposal procedures, PPE for technicians, and additional labor and materials. Must be handled in compliance with OSHA regulations.

definitions.definition: Time required to bring vehicle to full operating temperature for proper diagnostic testing or calibration procedures.
definitions.details: Many diagnostic procedures and calibrations require the vehicle to be at normal operating temperature. This is separate from drive cycle time.

definitions.definition: Time to clean vehicle of all broken glass from the collision. Depending on which glass was broken and to what extent.
definitions.details: May require R&I of dash and ducts, testing A/C to inspect vents, checking wiper blades for glass damage. Glass fragments must be removed from interior, exterior, and all crevices.

definitions.definition: Charge and maintain battery during the repair and calibration process.
definitions.details: Maintains charge during long repairs, prevents memory loss, keeps modules powered, avoids relearns. Methods include battery maintainer/charger, memory saver during disconnect, or external power supply.

definitions.definition: Need to adjust tire pressures for ADAS Calibrations, to aim lamps, and for all wheels to travel at the same speed.
definitions.details: Proper tire pressure is required for accurate ADAS calibration, TPMS reset, and affects vehicle height which impacts calibration targets. DOES NOT INCLUDE: Adjusting tire pressure per G11.

definitions.definition: Check Wheel Balance to determine if the wheel can be reused, repaired, or requires replacement with a new tire.
definitions.details: If wheel is found to be bent and unable to be balanced, new wheel is required. Balance check confirms wheel integrity after collision impact.

definitions.definition: Clean up or detailing of vehicle prior to delivery.
definitions.details: SPECIAL NOTATION - The items listed below apply to all labor procedures. If an estimated work time is not available for the items below, an on-the-spot evaluation is recommended to determine an appropriate estimated time.

definitions.definition: Refinish times do not include time which may be required to match color tints or defective finish textures on interior or exterior surfaces.
definitions.details: Color tinting and matching is a skilled process that varies by vehicle age, paint type, and sun exposure. Additional time for color development and matching is not included.

definitions.definition: A vehicle's ability to resist corrosion may be reduced if improper repairs are made and the manufacturer's corrosion protection is not restored. This may lead to future safety and/or resale value problems.
definitions.details: DOES NOT INCLUDE: Anti-corrosion material application. SPECIAL NOTATION: The items listed below apply to all labor procedures. If an estimated work time is not available, an on-the-spot evaluation is recommended. Anti-corrosion material restoration/application.

definitions.definition: Protect electrical connections from contaminant, debris and moisture. Sealing open electrical plugs.
definitions.details: When a connector/plug/wire or computer module is left open to the environment, especially in adverse weather, it can potentially allow moisture and debris into the electrical system and can cause issues with the vehicle. These issues may not show right away, the vehicle may take some time to recognize there is an issue in system.

definitions.term: Cover Interior and Protection during Repair
definitions.definition: Protection during repairs including cutting, welding, bonding, riveting. Protection from dust, dirt, etc. during repair process.
definitions.details: Interior protection prevents damage to seats, carpet, dash, and other interior components during structural repairs, welding, and other operations that produce debris or sparks.

definitions.definition: Used to determine if a panel is cracked. Great for cast aluminum components.
definitions.details: Dye penetrant testing reveals cracks that may not be visible to the naked eye. Especially useful on cast aluminum suspension components, wheels, and structural parts.

definitions.definition: SPECIAL NOTATION: Refinished panels may or may not require a varying amount of de-nibbing, a process used to remove small particles in final finish surface.
definitions.details: Each panel requiring de-nibbing (refinish or blend): HOOD, ROOF, TRUNK LID, SPOILER - first panel add up to 20% of full base refinish time, first panel add up to 10% for FENDER, DOOR, QUARTER PANEL, BUMPER COVER. Each additional panel add up to 5%.

definitions.definition: If vehicle becomes a total loss, this is to remove vehicle owner addresses, saved locations, phone numbers, garage door programming, etc.
definitions.details: Every vehicle is different and shop has to learn what options and which systems this vehicle has and then see what the customer has programmed in and learn to clear it. Privacy concern for vehicle owners.

definitions.definition: Non-included operation. Test drive vehicle prior to disassembly due to vehicle collision and immediately being towed. Vehicle needs to have a drive cycle performed to have DTC's occur prior to final road test after repairs have been complete.
definitions.details: Drive cycle allows vehicle computers to run through all monitors and set any pending codes. Required for proper pre-scan and post-scan verification.

definitions.definition: Test drive to confirm all operations are functioning properly.
definitions.details: DOES NOT INCLUDE per G11: Adjusting tire pressure, checking levelness of ground, creating targets, filling gas tank, R&I of parts for alignment, removing or replacing customer valuables from vehicles, repair of mounting surfaces. Road test dynamic aiming. Road test vehicle G14, G16, G17.

definitions.definition: Non-included operation. LABOR TIME DOES NOT INCLUDE: Reset electronic memory functions after battery disconnect.
definitions.details: SPECIAL NOTATION: The items listed below apply to all labor procedures. Examples include seat position memory, radio presets, mirror positions, window auto-up/down, idle relearn, and other module adaptations.

definitions.term: Feather Edge & Block Sand / Prime & Block
definitions.definition: Prime & Block (high build / primer-filler) is a required procedure that restores a repaired panel surface, including the joined areas of replaced welded panels, from 150-grit finish to New Undamaged condition.
definitions.details: It is MOTOR'S position that prime and block is a refinishing process best reserved for the judgement of an estimator/appraiser following a thorough on-the-spot evaluation of the specific vehicle and damage in question. DOES NOT INCLUDE: Anti-corrosion material application, filling, blocking, feather edging repaired panels, Prime & Block (high build/primer-filler).

definitions.definition: Flex additive mixing time, flex prep application, and material costs are not included.
definitions.details: DOES NOT INCLUDE: Anti-corrosion material application, filling, blocking, feather edging repaired panels. Flex Additive mixing time, flex prep application, material costs. Required when painting flexible parts like bumper covers.

definitions.definition: Any unclear glass, sensors, cameras, etc. can throw off calibrations by up to 1.5 degrees.
definitions.details: Windshield and camera lens must be clean for proper ADAS calibration. Dirt, debris, or film can affect camera and sensor accuracy, resulting in failed or inaccurate calibration.

definitions.definition: Used to clean and degrease surface before installing double-sided taped parts.
definitions.details: IPA (Isopropyl Alcohol) wipe removes oils, wax, and contaminants that prevent proper adhesion of tape-attached moldings, emblems, and trim pieces.

definitions.definition: Protection during DE-NIB, Wet/Dry Sand and Compound, protection from residue in jambs and moldings etc.
definitions.details: Parts removed for refinish must be protected from overspray, compound residue, and damage during the refinish process.

definitions.term: Mask Backside of Bumper for Radar
definitions.definition: Masking the backside of bumper covers in the radar sensor zone during refinish operations.
definitions.details: Vehicles with front radar sensors require careful masking of the radar zone on the backside of bumper covers. Paint in this area can affect radar performance and cause ADAS malfunctions.

definitions.definition: Mobile cart for Hybrid and EV platform is designed for high voltage safety as a top priority. A high voltage safety system is a set of measures and components designed to protect people and equipment from dangers associated with high voltage electrical systems.
definitions.details: PPE required: high voltage gloves, protective coated tools, barriers, safety hook, EV fire blankets, class D fire extinguisher, dedicated voltage meter, eye protection, isolated caps. Must be in compliance with EV Manufacturers and local regulations, norms, and standards. PPE must be suitable for purpose, used properly per manufacturer instructions, well maintained, and safely stored.

definitions.definition: The collision repair facility must have the equipment, training, and experience using the latest factory/industry information and procedures.
definitions.details: Ultimately, the safety and quality of any repair will depend not only on the equipment used and the procedure followed, but also on the skill and knowledge of the repair technicians, the steps they take to control repair quality and how well they check the details of the repair. Some repair operations may be beyond the capability of the repair shop - in this case the repair shop should not attempt the repair. Vehicles cannot be repaired properly without OEM procedures or research; this is a non-included operation and an on-the-spot evaluation.

definitions.definition: ESTIMATED WORK TIME DOES NOT INCLUDE: Waste disposal fees (all types).
definitions.details: SPECIAL NOTATION: The items listed below apply to all labor procedures. If an estimated work time is not available for the items below, an on-the-spot evaluation is recommended to determine an appropriate estimated time. Includes hazardous materials disposal.

definitions.definition: ESTIMATED WORK TIME DOES NOT INCLUDE: Material costs.
definitions.details: SPECIAL NOTATION: The items listed below apply to all labor procedures. If an estimated work time is not available, an on-the-spot evaluation is recommended. Material costs G9, G34. Paint or material costs G35, G36, G37. Materials are billed separately from labor.

definitions.definition: Scaffolding equipment required due to height of vehicle.
definitions.details: For large vehicles (box trucks, RVs, commercial vehicles), roof access on tall vehicles requires scaffolding. Safety equipment for height work and additional rental/setup cost not included.

definitions.definition: Pre-Scan - before disassembly. In-Process Scan - after reassembly but before calibrations. Post-Scan - after calibrations and test drive to verify all codes have been addressed and haven't returned.
definitions.details: All three scan types are not included in standard labor times. OEMs recommend pre and post scans on all collision repairs. In-process scan may be needed when reenergizing hybrid/EV vehicles during repair.
definitions.degResponse: DEG Inquiry 17537 - CCC defines in-process repair scan as 'any scan completed after pre-repair scan and before post-repair scan.' All three information providers (CCC, Mitchell and Audatex) consider this step to be NOT INCLUDED.

definitions.definition: Shock/Strut must have the gas discharged before disposal.
definitions.details: Methods for discharging gas can include: drill a hole, use a hacksaw to make a hole. Pressurized gas struts cannot be disposed of without proper depressurization.

definitions.term: Spray Out Cards / Test Spray Panel
definitions.definition: SPECIAL NOTATION: The following items or operations were not considered during the development of any published basic refinish operation times. If any of these items or operations are required, they should be considered by the estimator. DOES NOT INCLUDE: Test spray out panel.
definitions.details: Spray out cards are used to verify color match before applying to vehicle. Essential for metallic, pearl, and tri-coat colors.

definitions.definition: CAUTION: Deployed front airbags having two initiators (squibs) in the airbag inflator may or may not have live pyrotechnic material within the inflator. Do not dispose of these airbags unless you are certain of complete deployment.
definitions.details: All damaged, ineffective, or non-deployed SRS components which are replaced must be handled and disposed of properly. If an airbag or seat belt tensioner unit is ineffective or damaged and non-deployed, refer to the Hazardous Substance Control System. Dispose of all non-deployed and deployed airbags and seat belt tensioners in a manner consistent with state, provincial, local and federal regulations. Water Shield note: if vehicle has Impact Sensor in door, water shield may need replacement due to air pressure difference once removed.

definitions.definition: Staging and securing parts for the refinish process.
definitions.details: This is a non-included operation. Includes setting up fixtures, stands, and securing parts properly for spray application.

definitions.definition: ESTIMATED WORK TIME DOES NOT INCLUDE: Steering Angle Sensor Recalibration.
definitions.details: SPECIAL NOTATION: The items listed below apply to all labor procedures. If an estimated work time is not available for the items below, an on-the-spot evaluation is recommended to determine an appropriate estimated time. Required after wheel alignment, suspension work, steering component replacement.

definitions.definition: Low battery voltage and/or repair procedures may inadvertently set multiple DTC's. Clear DTC's and determine which ones reset after battery voltage is stabilized.
definitions.details: Weak battery can cause false diagnostic codes. Testing confirms battery health before scans and prevents diagnostic issues.

definitions.definition: Proper disposal of TPMS (Tire Pressure Monitoring System) sensors.
definitions.details: Process includes: Remove battery, remove urethane, cut battery terminal. TPMS sensors contain batteries that require proper disposal procedures.

definitions.definition: ESTIMATED WORK TIME DOES NOT INCLUDE: Trial Fit of a component to verify dimensional accuracy of adjoining parts.
definitions.details: SPECIAL NOTATION: The items listed below apply to all labor procedures. Repair times include just one test fit of a component. Second or subsequent fitment of a component to the vehicle, for any reason, is not included.

definitions.definition: Per Motors GTE Page G35: Only one solvent wash is included in the basic clear coat application.
definitions.details: Refinish times are based on new undamaged panels, therefore any additional washes required during the repair of the panel is non-included in any labor time.

definitions.definition: ESTIMATED WORK TIME DOES NOT INCLUDE: Access to repair information/subscription cost, waste disposal fees (all types).
definitions.details: SPECIAL NOTATION: The items listed below apply to all labor procedures. If an estimated work time is not available, an on-the-spot evaluation is recommended to determine an appropriate estimated time.

definitions.definition: WET/DRY SAND, RUB-OUT & BUFF SPECIAL NOTATION: Refinished panels may or may not require a varying amount of wet sanding, compound rub-out or buffing operations in order to match original finish texture.
definitions.details: Each panel requiring wet sand, rub-out and/or buff (refinish or blend) - add 30% of full base refinish time.

definitions.definition: Additional seam sealer application beyond what was removed during part removal. Includes duplicating OEM caulk/seam sealer on hood, door, trunk lid, liftgate, tail gate, and pickup box.
definitions.details: The estimated work time includes replacing all seam sealer that was removed due to removal of part. Any additional seam sealer replacement requires an on-the-spot evaluation.
definitions.degResponse: DEG Inquiry 23010 - CCC/MOTOR Response: 'The estimated work time includes replacing all seam sealer that was removed due to removal of part. Any additional seam sealer replacement would need to be an on the spot evaluation.'

definitions.definition: Removal and re-application of seam sealer/protective coatings over fasteners on bolt-on parts such as fenders, doors, and hoods.
definitions.details: Some vehicles have seam sealer/protective coatings over fasteners on bolt-on parts which require additional labor to remove and reapply. The process of removing seam sealer and re-applying as well as refinishing is NOT included unless specifically noted.
definitions.degResponse: Mitchell: 'The removal and installation of caulking/seam sealer for factory application on a fender/bolts has not been factored into the labor allowance.' Audatex: 'Removal of debris, grease, corrosion, protective coatings, or other materials impeding replacement, R&I, or refinishing of parts is not included.'

definitions.definition: A landmark 2022 study by the Society of Collision Repair Specialists (SCRS) that proved blend refinish operations take MORE time than full panel refinish, not less as the estimating databases calculate.
definitions.details: The study involved 5 major paint manufacturers (AkzoNobel, Axalta, BASF, PPG, Sherwin-Williams) at the GFS Center for Excellence in Wisconsin. Using 2018 Ford F-150 parts with DEKRA as third-party auditor, the study found blend time was 31.59% GREATER than full refinish time. This directly contradicts the industry standard where all three estimating systems (CCC/MOTOR, Mitchell, Audatex) calculate blend at 50% of full refinish time. Aaron Schulenburg, SCRS Executive Director stated: 'These results very clearly establish that performing the processes necessary to blend a panel does not take less labor than the processes to fully refinish a panel.'

definitions.definition: In addition to the base and mid coat applications of a 3-Stage process, the 4-Stage refinish process may require an additional ground coat. Some paint manufacturers use a tinted clear coat in addition to the final non-tinted clear coat.
definitions.details: These additional steps are not included and should be considered when developing an estimate. 4-stage adds approximately 100% to the base refinish time (add 25% of full refinish time to each panel/part for 4-stage).

definitions.definition: Adhesion promoter may not be required for primed parts. Many replacement bumper covers are supplied primed from the maker. A solvent test should be performed to ensure the primer does not dissolve when solvent is applied.
definitions.details: An adhesion promoter may be required for parts that are not supplied primed. In addition, adhesion promoter may be required on bare areas of repaired parts. Adhesion promoter should not be applied to adjacent areas where the original coatings are left intact. Typical labor: 0.3 refinish.

definitions.definition: Refinish the backside of panels. Applies to: Aperture, Fender, Floor, Quarter, Rear body panels.
definitions.details: Calculated as half the base refinish time of the panel. When painting the backside of a panel such as a quarter panel or fender, the color is usually different than the exterior. This requires a second color tint, second spray out card, and a 2K product for proper corrosion protection. Add 0.2 refinish for masking front side of panel when spraying the inside.

definitions.definition: Cut open a panel so you can see additional damage. Occurs when the outer panel is damaged beyond repair and the inner panel needs to be inspected.
definitions.details: Prior to cutting the outer panel, document the damage with all photos. Add the product used to make the access hole (saw blade, cutting wheel, etc.). Typical labor: 1.0 body. Also applies when door/hood/liftgate latch area is damaged and the part cannot be opened.

definitions.definition: Also known as spot sealer. Used to prevent rust on the mating surfaces of spot welds.
definitions.details: FCA US LLC (Dodge, Chrysler, etc.) does not recommend the use of any type of weld-thru primer during repairs. Weld-bonding with corrosion protecting adhesives or sealers, along with final application of inner panel corrosion protection is the proper method. VW has their own specification. Copper weld through primer is not recommended. Zinc weld through primer is acceptable. Must be stored upside down or sideways to prevent clogging. Typical material cost: $10.

definitions.definition: Occurs when you replace a welded body panel and have to perform a repair to the mating panels. Mating panels are what touches the replacement panel - can include the visual exterior panel and the inner panel.
definitions.details: Small Panel WZR: 0.5 body repair. Large Panel or two mating surfaces: 1.0 body repair. If the vehicle is welded from factory and the replacement part is bonded and riveted, you still have to perform weld zone repair as the vehicle was once welded. If the panel is also damaged, add repair units for the repair AND repair units for the weld zone repair as it is two separate operations.
definitions.degResponse: DEG Inquiry 13434: After review of the concern, it has been determined that covering the exterior, interior and components of the vehicle to prevent weld spark damage is not included with welded panel replacement estimated work times.

definitions.definition: Testing structural welds by destructive testing methods to verify weld quality.
definitions.details: Typical labor: 1.0 body per weld tested. Add 0.5 body for each additional weld tested. Types include: Butt weld, MIG/MAG weld, Plug weld, Spot weld. Audi requires saving test welds for 7 years.

definitions.definition: Used to aid in repairing dents. Less intrusive than replacing a panel. Use cutting wheel to cut off studs after pulling.
definitions.details: Tips: Scuff the side of the tab that touches the panel. Reusable tabs - to install and remove do it straight. To remove, twist it. Clean with solvent and degrease prior to brushing. Use stainless steel brush (white) to see pure aluminum. Brush only where the stud is going. Do not pull tabs as that will remove material and warp the panel. Do not use Roloc as it uses too much heat. Typical labor: 0.5 body. Typical cost: $25.

definitions.definition: Apply cavity wax to enclosed structural areas after panel replacement or repair.
definitions.details: 3 mils or 3 passes to be effective. Any repaired panel needs to have cavity wax added. Industry guideline: 5 grams per body labor hour, or 1 can per every 100 hours, or 1 can per week. Typical labor: 0.3 body per panel.

definitions.definition: Preparation required when repairing a bumper or plastic part that requires repair down to the actual raw plastic.
definitions.details: Required when existing coatings are removed down to bare plastic substrate. Includes cleaning, scuffing, and adhesion promoter application. Typical labor: 0.5 refinish.

definitions.definition: Paintable product which is different from Gravel Guard. Most cars have this. This is usually with a car whose rocker molding is touching the quarter panel.
definitions.details: This is sprayed and then gets painted over. Different from chip guard and gravel guard. Typical labor: 0.5 refinish. Typical mask for undercoat: 0.5 refinish.

definitions.definition: Used to remove static from parts prior to painting.
definitions.details: Apply 0.1 refinish per part. Static electricity attracts dust and contaminants to parts before painting. Static gun neutralizes the charge for cleaner paint application. Typical cost: $14.

definitions.definition: All electronics within welding distance must be removed to prevent damage during welding operations.
definitions.details: Labor varies depending on vehicle, typically 0.5-2.0 mechanical. Porsche: If welding on a Porsche, remove plug connections on engine, transmission and airbag control units per procedure. Toyota requires 18 inches clearance per Toyota Collision Damage Repair Precautions. Be sure to remove all speakers and SRS components (airbags, seatbelts, sensors) in the area.

definitions.definition: Welder Setup Time, Power up, Power down per welder used.
definitions.details: Typical labor: 0.5 body per welder. Typical cost: $25 per welder setup. May require separate setup for: Spot welder, Plug welder/MIG/MAG, Aluminum welder, Steel welder. Note the amount of welders required to repair the vehicle.

definitions.definition: Consumable materials required for welding operations.
definitions.details: Includes: Coatings required by OEM for welding, cost of OEM specific welding coupons, labor for protecting the vehicle from welding and sparks, shielding gas, spark deflection material, welding tips, welding wire (Bohler Union x96, Brazin wire, Er70s-6, OEM specific Alu wires, etc.). Typical cost: $25.

definitions.definition: If you cannot pull the vehicle due to location, vehicle construction, or manufacturer guidelines, you can hammer the part out to restore dimensions and relieve pressure prior to removing and replacing the panel.
definitions.details: Typical labor: 1.0-2.0 body. Required when pressure was noted upon releasing or removing damaged components. Inertia travels - if removing a part such as an impact bar and you notice pressure upon release, there may be more damage that you may not see.

definitions.definition: Apply primer to removed glass area. Procedure must state to use OEM or equivalent as stated.
definitions.details: Body Primer for pinchweld and Glass Primer for bonding surface. Large glass: $20 and 0.6 body. Small glass: $10 and 0.3 body. Using the OEM urethane or equivalent is critical - in an accident, airbags deploy and bounce off the windshield. If incorrect urethane was used, the airbag may go through the windshield instead of protecting occupants.

definitions.definition: Apply OEM urethane or equivalent as stated in OEM Repair Procedures for glass installation.
definitions.details: Large glass urethane: 0.8 body. Small glass urethane: 0.5 body. OEM price varies, equivalent non-OEM typically $30 large glass or $20 small glass. Using OEM urethane or equivalent is highly important for crash safety.

definitions.definition: Carbon Fiber-Reinforced Plastics (CFRPs) are Fiber-Reinforced Plastics (FRP) where the fibers are made of carbon. These are lightweight, high-strength materials used in performance and luxury vehicles.
definitions.details: CFRPs are used for body panels, structural reinforcements, drive shafts, suspension parts, and even passenger cells. Woven carbon fiber is stronger and lighter than chopped carbon fiber. Carbon fiber dust is conductive and can short circuit electrical tools. Raw carbon fiber can cause galvanic corrosion when in contact with metal, but this is eliminated once embedded in resin. All repairs must follow OEM procedures.

definitions.definition: Sheet Molded Compound (SMC) is smooth on both sides with no visible fibers unless the part is cracked. SMC parts cannot be repaired using fiberglass repair materials or plastic welders.
definitions.details: DO NOT use fiberglass repair materials with SMC because of the fillers and mold release agents that cause adhesion problems. SMC requires both sides of the part to be finish sanded. SMC is used for weight reduction and complex shapes. When repairing SMC: use composite repair adhesive, not fiberglass resin. Products: Fibre Glast Epoxy Resin, Evercoat Fibertech (Gorilla hair), Dynatron Fiberglass Filler. Allow proper dry time. Add primer at 25% of repair unit as refinish.

definitions.definition: FRP is a general term for Fiber-Reinforced Plastics representing any fiber a composite contains. Composites can contain glass fibers or carbon fibers.
definitions.details: FRP Backing can be made of fiberglass cloth or backing tape. General recommendation is 1/2 inch to 2 inch overlap on all sides of damage. Traditional fiberglass is made by saturating fiberglass cloth or mat with polyester resin. Fiberglass parts usually start with a gel coat applied to a waxed mold, then fiberglass and resin are hand or spray applied. Repairs can be done using polyester resin and fiberglass cloth or mat.

definitions.definition: When working with composites, proper safety equipment is critical. Fractured or splintered composites, particularly carbon fiber, can be extremely sharp.
definitions.details: Required PPE: Leather gloves when damaged fibers are exposed. NIOSH P100 combination respirator when grinding or cutting carbon fiber (prevents fibers and gases from lungs). Safety glasses. Composite dust from sanding, cutting, or grinding is a lung and eye irritant. Perform repairs in properly ventilated area. Review product-specific Safety Data Sheets (SDS) for each material used.

definitions.definition: One-sided cosmetic repairs for composites primarily include scratches or gouges that do not penetrate through to the backside.
definitions.details: Steps: 1) Clean the repair area. 2) Scuff or sand the damaged area. 3) Apply composite repair adhesive or filler. 4) Sand to contour after product has cured. 5) Finish with paint maker's recommended grit for repair. Do not use solvents as fibers can wick solvents into the FRP.

definitions.definition: Two-sided repairs include cracks, tears, or punctures that go through to the backside or have damage to the fibers.
definitions.details: Steps: 1) Remove any loose or damaged fibers with air saw or die grinder (cut a circle around damaged area). 2) Taper the front side of the repair. 3) Apply aluminum tape to front side to prevent material falling through. 4) Apply composite repair adhesive to backside repair area. 5) Press backing material (fiberglass cloth or tape, 1/2-2 inch overlap) into adhesive with firm pressure. 6) Apply another layer of adhesive over backing, covering all edges. 7) Allow to cure, remove aluminum tape. 8) Sand front side creating repair taper. 9) Apply adhesive and finish. Take care not to damage fibers. Fibers can wick solvents - avoid solvent use.

definitions.definition: A pyramid patch is an alternative method of composite repair involving a patch built up with alternate layers of repair product and fiber cloth or mesh.
definitions.details: Steps: 1) Clean and scuff backside repair area, vacuum dust. 2) Apply aluminum tape to front side. 3) Apply composite repair adhesive to backside backing, cover entire patch and allow to cure. 4) Remove aluminum tape from front, sand creating repair taper. 5) Apply layer of adhesive, then cloth/mesh, then another layer of adhesive. 6) Continue building layers in pyramid fashion. 7) Sand to contour after curing. Note: Some manufacturers have special instructions for their composite repair adhesive.

definitions.definition: Carbon fiber presents unique repair challenges that may make replacement necessary instead of repair.
definitions.details: Cannot repair if damage extends to an edge. Cannot duplicate exposed carbon fiber weave if cracked (you cannot recreate the exact weave pattern of a tool-faced surface). Cannot buff if damage goes through clearcoat on tool-faced surfaces. Carbon fiber dust can short circuit electrical tools (conductive material). Raw carbon fiber causes galvanic corrosion with metal contact. Manufacturing uses autoclave process: 300Â°F heat and 100 psi pressure with epoxy, vinyl ester, polyester, or nylon resins.

definitions.definition: Several types of failure can occur with composite materials that require different inspection and repair approaches.
definitions.details: DELAMINATION: One ply separates from another ply. May result from low-energy impact or manufacturing defect. Sometimes visible if near surface. DISBONDING: A ply separates from dissimilar material or core material. CORE DAMAGE: Damage to core material of part, can happen along with any other damage type. CRACKING/PUNCTURES: High energy impacts crack or puncture parts - easiest failure type to see. Inspect backside for secondary damage. TAP TEST: Technician taps across suspected damage area listening for tone change, marks where tone changes to identify damage boundaries.

definitions.definition: Identifying carbon fiber parts is important as repair procedures differ significantly from other materials.
definitions.details: CHOPPED CARBON FIBER: Looks similar to fine fibered fiberglass with very fine fibers in random directions. Extremely thin, light, and stronger than fiberglass. May look similar to SMC but contains carbon fibers. WOVEN CARBON FIBER: Lighter and stronger than chopped. Distinctive weave pattern visible. Structural and non-structural parts can be carbon fiber. Not limited to body panels - can include drive shafts, suspension parts, passenger cell, structural reinforcements, engine/transmission parts. PAINTED PANELS: May be easy to miss - use same identification steps as traditional composites.

definitions.definition: Several repair options exist for carbon fiber and composites depending on damage type and OEM procedures.
definitions.details: COSMETIC/BUFFING: Surface scratches and damage that can be buffed out easily. COMPOSITE-TYPE REPAIR: Uses same products and techniques as SMC repairs (pyramid patch). Note: This can ONLY be used for cosmetic applications because repairs are weaker than carbon fiber and do not meet same performance standards. VACUUM BAGGING: Advanced repair method with many different options and steps. Consult OEM procedures. Always follow original equipment manufacturer (OEM) procedures. Repairs act as secondary bond depending on repair materials adhering to original materials - use materials designed for the specific composite being repaired.

definitions.definition: Specific products recommended for composite and SMC/carbon fiber repairs.
definitions.details: RESIN: Fibre Glast Epoxy Resin & Epoxy Cure. FIBERGLASS: Fiberglass Mat (white bag, red letters). GORILLA HAIR: Evercoat Fibertech 100633 - Reinforced Repair Compound Filler. FILLER: Dynatron Fiberglass Filler. PRIMER: Add at 25% repair unit as refinish unit, treat same as other parts. STRUCTURAL ADHESIVES (GM approved): Fusor 2098, 3M 07333, Pilogrip 5770P, SEM 39757 Structural Impact Resistant Adhesive. Always verify current OEM approved products list.

definitions.term: Stage and Secure Carbon Fiber for Refinish
definitions.definition: Stage and secure carbon fiber molding for refinish is a non-included operation per DEG inquiry.
definitions.details: Carbon fiber parts require special handling and staging for refinish operations. This includes proper positioning, securing, and preparation specific to carbon fiber substrates. Also consider: DE-NIB, Wet/Dry Sand, Rub-Out and Buff for carbon fiber parts.

definitions.definition: Remove and Install (R&I) is the labor operation of removing a part from the vehicle and reinstalling the SAME part back onto the vehicle. The part is not replaced - it is temporarily removed to gain access to other components or repair areas.
definitions.details: R&I is used when a part must be removed to access damage behind it, but the part itself is undamaged and will be reused. Examples: R&I headlamp to access fender bolts, R&I grille to access radiator support, R&I door panel to access window regulator. R&I times are typically lower than R&R (Replace) times because no new part installation adjustments are needed. The part comes off and goes back in the same position.

definitions.definition: Replace (Repl or R&R - Remove and Replace) is the labor operation of removing a damaged part from the vehicle and installing a NEW replacement part. The original part is discarded and a new part takes its place.
definitions.details: Replace operations include removal of the old part, installation of the new part, and any necessary adjustments for proper fit and alignment. Replace times are typically higher than R&I times because new parts may require adjustments, transfers of components, and alignment. Example: Replace fender means removing the damaged fender and installing a brand new fender. Does NOT include refinish - that is a separate operation.

definitions.definition: Repair (Rpr) is the labor operation of restoring a damaged part to its original condition without replacing it. The technician uses various techniques to fix dents, straighten metal, fill imperfections, and prepare the surface for refinish.
definitions.details: Repair labor is measured in repair units or hours. The estimating systems provide repair time based on damage severity. Light damage may be 1-2 hours, moderate 3-4 hours, heavy repair can be 6+ hours. Repair operations include: metal straightening, filling, sanding, grinding welds, and preparing for prime/paint. Does NOT include refinish time - that is calculated separately. When repair time exceeds replacement cost, the part should be replaced instead.

definitions.definition: Blend is a refinish operation where color is gradually faded into an adjacent panel to achieve an invisible color match. Instead of painting the entire adjacent panel, the painter applies color that tapers off, blending into the existing finish.
definitions.details: Blending is required when painting one panel would create a visible color difference with adjacent panels due to paint fade, metallic orientation, or pearl coat variations. All estimating systems calculate blend at 50% of full refinish time, HOWEVER the 2022 SCRS Blend Study proved blend actually takes 31.59% MORE time than full refinish due to the additional skill and technique required. Blend clearcoat is separate from blend basecoat. Blend may not be possible on matte finishes or certain specialty paints.

definitions.definition: Refinish is the labor operation of applying paint to a vehicle panel. This includes surface preparation, masking, priming, application of basecoat color, and clearcoat. Refinish restores the vehicle's appearance after body repairs or part replacement.
definitions.details: Refinish labor is separate from body repair labor. A panel may need both repair AND refinish. Refinish times are based on panels being in 'new undamaged condition' - any additional prep for repaired panels is extra. Refinish operations include: clean/scuff, mask, apply sealer/primer, apply basecoat, apply clearcoat, unmask. NOT included: prime & block, de-nib, wet sand/buff, color tint, 3-stage/4-stage additional coats, flex additive, blend into adjacent panels.

definitions.definition: Paintless Dent Repair (PDR) is a specialized technique for removing minor dents and dings from vehicle body panels without affecting the factory paint finish. Technicians use specialized tools to massage the metal back to its original shape from behind the panel.
definitions.details: PDR is ideal for door dings, hail damage, and minor dents where the paint is not cracked or damaged. Benefits: preserves factory paint/warranty, faster than traditional repair, often more cost-effective, maintains vehicle value. Limitations: cannot repair dents with paint damage, sharp creases, or dents on panel edges. PDR technicians are specialists - this work is often sublet. PDR pricing is typically per dent or per panel, not hourly labor.

definitions.definition: Sublet refers to work that is sent out to a third-party specialist or vendor rather than being performed in-house at the body shop. The shop contracts with outside specialists for services that require special equipment, certification, or expertise.
definitions.details: Common sublet operations include: wheel alignment, ADAS calibrations, A/C service, glass replacement, PDR (paintless dent repair), upholstery repair, key programming, mechanical repairs, frame/structural measuring. Sublet charges are typically invoiced at cost plus a markup (commonly 20-25%) to cover the shop's time coordinating the work. Sublet invoices should be included with the repair documentation. Some insurers require prior authorization for sublet work over certain dollar amounts.
